secondary_meaning: based on assumed conditions, not proven
common_collocations: scénario hypothétique, question hypothétique, cas hypothétique
image_context: Abstract logical reasoning representing hypothetical scenarios
example_1: Nous devons examiner ce scénario hypothétique avec prudence avant de conclure.
example_1_translation: We must examine this hypothetical scenario with caution before concluding.
example_2: L'argument repose sur une prémisse hypothétique non démontrée par l'expérience.
example_2_translation: The argument rests on an undemonstrated hypothetical premise based on experience.
example_3: Posons un cas hypothétique pour mieux comprendre ce dilemme éthique.
example_3_translation: Let us pose a hypothetical case to better understand this ethical dilemma.
pronunciation_tips: i-po-tè-tik. Stress on third syllable: i-po-TÈ-tik
memory_hook: Hypothétique - from hypothèse (hypothesis) - something assumed but not yet proven
common_mistakes: Often confused with 'théorique' - hypothetical is specifically about assumed conditions, theoretical is broader

secondary_meaning: resulting from physician action or medical procedure
usage_notes: Use with medical conditions, complications, effects
common_collocations: effet iatrogène, complication iatrogène, pathologie iatrogène
synonyms: provoqué par le traitement, d'origine médicale
image_context: Medical environment representing complications from treatment
example_1: La dépression peut être iatrogène lorsqu'elle résulte d'effets secondaires médicamenteux graves.
example_1_translation: Depression can be iatrogenic when it results from serious drug side effects.
example_2: Les complications iatrogènes sont un enjeu majeur de la sécurité des patients en hôpital.
example_2_translation: Iatrogenic complications are a major issue in patient safety in hospitals.
example_3: Cette maladie était iatrogène, causée par l'intervention chirurgicale précédente.
example_3_translation: This disease was iatrogenic, caused by the previous surgical intervention.
pronunciation_tips: ya-tro-jè-n. Medical term from Greek iatros (physician) + genos (origin)
memory_hook: Iatrogène - iatros (physician in Greek) + gène (origin) - caused by the doctor
common_mistakes: Not to be confused with 'allergic' - iatrogenic is specifically about medical treatment causing harm

secondary_meaning: an unusual reaction or sensitivity in a particular individual
usage_notes: Use with 'propre à', 'caractéristique de', describing unique traits
common_collocations: idiosyncrasie personnelle, idiosyncrasie comportementale, propre idiosyncrasie
example_1: Chaque individu possède ses propres idiosyncrasies comportementales distinctes.
example_1_translation: Each individual has their own distinct behavioral idiosyncrasies.
example_2: L'idiosyncrasie du patient doit être prise en compte dans la conception du traitement.
example_2_translation: The patient's idiosyncrasy must be taken into account in treatment design.
example_3: L'une de ses idiosyncrasies était de collectionner des cartes postales anciennes.
example_3_translation: One of his idiosyncrasies was collecting old postcards.
pronunciation_tips: i-djo-sèn-kra-zi. Five syllables with stress on penultimate syllable
memory_hook: Idiosyncrasie - from Greek idios (own) + synkrasis (mixture) - a unique mix of traits
common_mistakes: Often used to describe negative traits, but it's neutral - simply means individual characteristic

primary_meaning: nepotism, favoritism shown to relatives in appointments
secondary_meaning: systematic practice of giving jobs or advantages to family members
usage_notes: Use with accusations, systems, practices in government or business
common_collocations: accusation de népotisme, combat contre le népotisme, politique du népotisme
image_context: Political corruption symbols representing nepotism and family favoritism
example_1: Le népotisme dans l'administration publique sème la corruption et l'injustice.
example_1_translation: Nepotism in public administration sows corruption and injustice.
example_2: L'accusation de népotisme a entraîné la démission du ministre et sa family.
example_2_translation: The accusation of nepotism led to the minister's resignation and his family's scandal.
example_3: Cet empire familial prospérait grâce au népotisme organisé dans les hauts postes.
example_3_translation: This family empire thrived thanks to organized nepotism in top positions.
pronunciation_tips: nè-po-tism. Three syllables with stress on first: NÈ-po-tism
memory_hook: Népotisme - from Latin nepos (nephew) - favoring nephews and relatives
common_mistakes: Sometimes spelled néopotisme incorrectly - correct form is népotisme

primary_meaning: depreciation, amortization of asset value over time
secondary_meaning: gradual reduction in debt through periodic payments
usage_notes: Use in accounting, finance, real estate contexts
common_collocations: tableau d'amortissement, plan d'amortissement, amortissement linéaire
synonyms: dépréciation, paiement échelonné, amortissement du capital
image_context: Financial charts and depreciation schedules representing systematic payment
example_1: L'amortissement permet de répartir le coût d'un investissement sur sa durée utile.
example_1_translation: Amortization allows distributing the cost of an investment over its useful life.
example_2: Les tableaux d'amortissement sont essentiels pour la gestion financière d'une entreprise.
example_2_translation: Amortization schedules are essential for the financial management of a company.
example_3: Le plan d'amortissement de ce crédit s'étale sur vingt ans.
example_3_translation: The amortization plan for this loan extends over twenty years.
pronunciation_tips: a-mor-tis-maN. Four syllables with nasal ending on the last syllable
memory_hook: Amortissement - from amortir (to kill, extinguish) - systematically killing off a debt
common_mistakes: Should not be confused with 'insurance' (assurance) - amortissement is specifically financial

primary_meaning: epistemology, philosophical study of knowledge and its foundations
secondary_meaning: a system of knowing or understanding used in a particular field
usage_notes: Use in academic, philosophical contexts with 'de', 'et'
common_collocations: épistémologie scientifique, épistémologie sociale, épistémologie féministe
synonyms: théorie de la connaissance, gnoseologie, théorie du savoir
image_context: Books and knowledge symbols representing epistemology and philosophy of knowledge
example_1: L'épistémologie examine les fondements et les limites de la connaissance scientifique.
example_1_translation: Epistemology examines the foundations and limits of scientific knowledge.
example_2: Cette approche soulève des questions épistémologiques fondamentales sur la nature du savoir.
example_2_translation: This approach raises fundamental epistemological questions about the nature of knowledge.
example_3: Kant a révolutionné l'épistémologie en montrant que l'esprit structure l'expérience.
example_3_translation: Kant revolutionized epistemology by showing that the mind structures experience.
pronunciation_tips: ép-is-té-mo-lo-ji. Six syllables with stress on third: é-pis-TÉ-mo-lo-ji
memory_hook: Épistémologie - episteme (knowledge in Greek) + logia (study) - study of knowledge
common_mistakes: Often confused with 'ontologie' - epistemology is about how we know, ontology is about what exists

primary_meaning: pathological, relating to disease or abnormal functioning
usage_notes: Use with diseases, conditions, behaviors in medical or psychological context
common_collocations: mensonge pathologique, peur pathologique, comportement pathologique
image_context: Medical imagery representing pathological conditions and disease
example_1: Le mensonge pathologique est un symptôme de certains troubles graves de la personnalité.
example_1_translation: Pathological lying is a symptom of certain serious personality disorders.
example_2: Une peur pathologique peut nécessiter une intervention thérapeutique professionnelle urgente.
example_2_translation: A pathological fear may require urgent professional therapeutic intervention.
example_3: L'anatomopathologie révèle des changements pathologiques dans les tissus examinés.
example_3_translation: Anatomical pathology reveals pathological changes in the examined tissues.
pronunciation_tips: pa-to-lo-jik. Four syllables with stress on second: pa-TO-lo-jik
memory_hook: Pathologique - from pathos (disease in Greek) + logos (study) - relating to disease
common_mistakes: Should not simply mean 'bad' - it specifically refers to disease or abnormality

primary_meaning: lability, tendency toward rapid emotional changes or instability
secondary_meaning: readiness to change; instability of any kind
usage_notes: Use in psychology, medicine, or scientific contexts
common_collocations: labilité émotionnelle, labilité du marché, labilité psychique
image_context: Unstable emotional states and fluctuating conditions representing lability
example_1: La labilité émotionnelle caractérise certains troubles psychologiques graves.
example_1_translation: Emotional lability characterizes certain serious psychological disorders.
example_2: La labilité du marché financier reflète l'incertitude économique croissante.
example_2_translation: Market lability reflects growing economic uncertainty.
example_3: Ses sautes d'humeur reflétaient une labilité émotionnelle sous-jacente.
example_3_translation: His mood swings reflected underlying emotional lability.
pronunciation_tips: la-bi-li-té. Four syllables with stress on last: la-bi-li-TÉ
memory_hook: Labilité - from Latin labilis (ready to slip) - things that easily slip or change
common_mistakes: Often used only in psychology, but applies to any unstable or changeable situation

primary_meaning: plethora, an excess or overabundance of something
secondary_meaning: an excessive supply that causes problems
usage_notes: Use with 'de' when indicating the abundant thing, often with criticism
common_collocations: pléthore d'études, pléthore de règlements, pléthore d'informations
image_context: Overflowing abundance representing plethora and excess
example_1: Une pléthore d'études confirment cette hypothèse scientifique établie.
example_1_translation: A plethora of studies confirm this established scientific hypothesis.
example_2: La pléthore de réglementations paralyse parfois l'innovation et l'entrepreneuriat.
example_2_translation: A plethora of regulations sometimes paralyzes innovation and entrepreneurship.
example_3: Face à la pléthore d'options disponibles, les consommateurs se sentent perdus.
example_3_translation: Faced with a plethora of available options, consumers feel lost.
pronunciation_tips: plé-thor. Two syllables with stress on first: PLÉ-thor
memory_hook: Pléthore - from Greek plethora (excess fullness) - overflowing abundance
common_mistakes: Sometimes confused with 'profusion' - pléthore suggests problematic excess

primary_meaning: antinomian, relating to contradictions within a system
usage_notes: Use in philosophical contexts when discussing paradoxes or contradictions
common_collocations: principes antinomiques, contradictions antinomiques, positions antinomiques
image_context: Conflicting forces and opposing principles representing antinomies
example_1: Ces deux principes apparaissent antinomiques et donc logiquement incompatibles.
example_1_translation: These two principles appear antinomian and thus logically incompatible.
example_2: La pensée kantienne explore systématiquement les contradictions antinomiques de la raison.
example_2_translation: Kantian thought systematically explores the antinomical contradictions of reason.
example_3: La liberté et la nécessité sont souvent considérées comme antinomiques.
example_3_translation: Freedom and necessity are often considered antinomical.
pronunciation_tips: an-ti-no-mik. Four syllables with stress on second: an-TI-no-mik
memory_hook: Antinomique - anti (against) + nomos (law) - laws that contradict each other
common_mistakes: Not to be confused with 'anomalous' - antinomical specifically means contradictory

primary_meaning: capitalization, total market value of a company's shares
secondary_meaning: the rate of return at which future income is valued
usage_notes: Use with 'boursière' for stock market cap, in financial evaluation
common_collocations: capitalisation boursière, capitalisation de marché, taux de capitalisation
image_context: Stock market and financial capitalization charts and graphs
example_1: La capitalisation boursière de l'entreprise a doublé au cours de cette année.
example_1_translation: The company's market capitalization has doubled in the course of this year.
example_2: Le taux de capitalisation est un outil d'évaluation utilisé pour les immeubles.
example_2_translation: The capitalization rate is a valuation tool used for real estate properties.
example_3: Malgré les crises, la capitalisation globale des marchés se maintient.
example_3_translation: Despite crises, the overall capitalization of markets remains stable.
pronunciation_tips: ka-pi-ta-li-za-sjON. Six syllables with stress on last nasal syllable
memory_hook: Capitalisation - from capital - the total value of all capital (shares/assets)
common_mistakes: Should not be confused with 'capitalization' meaning use of capital letters

primary_meaning: catastrophism, belief that major changes occur through catastrophic events
secondary_meaning: the practice of predicting or expecting catastrophe
usage_notes: Use in philosophical, environmental, or historical contexts
common_collocations: catastrophisme environnemental, catastrophisme politique, logique catastrophiste
image_context: Apocalyptic imagery and catastrophic scenarios representing catastrophism
example_1: Le catastrophisme environnemental conduit parfois à l'inaction paradoxale.
example_1_translation: Environmental catastrophism sometimes leads to paradoxical inaction.
example_2: L'histoire ne suit pas nécessairement une logique catastrophiste ou progressive.
example_2_translation: History does not necessarily follow a catastrophist or progressive logic.
example_3: Le catastrophisme en géologie explique les changements brutaux de la Terre.
example_3_translation: Catastrophism in geology explains the abrupt changes of the Earth.
pronunciation_tips: ka-tas-tro-fism. Four syllables with stress on third: ka-tas-TRO-fism
memory_hook: Catastrophisme - catastrophe + isme (belief system) - belief in catastrophic change
common_mistakes: Catastrophism is a scientific theory, not necessarily pessimism

primary_meaning: iatrogenesis, disease or condition caused by medical treatment
secondary_meaning: the process of becoming ill due to medical intervention
usage_notes: Use in medical and healthcare safety discussions
common_collocations: prévention de l'iatrogénie, risque d'iatrogénie, événement indésirable iatrogénique
synonyms: accident iatrogène, complication médicale, effet indésirable
image_context: Medical care and patient safety measures representing iatrogenesis prevention
example_1: L'iatrogénie représente un risque considérable en médecine moderne et contemporaine.
example_1_translation: Iatrogenesis represents a considerable risk in modern and contemporary medicine.
example_2: La prévention de l'iatrogénie nécessite une vigilance constante de tous les professionnels.
example_2_translation: Prevention of iatrogenesis requires constant vigilance from all professionals.
example_3: Ce patient a souffert d'une iatrogénie suite à une erreur de diagnostic.
example_3_translation: This patient suffered from iatrogenesis following a diagnostic error.
pronunciation_tips: ya-tro-jé-ni. Four syllables with stress on third: ya-tro-JÉ-ni
memory_hook: Iatrogénie - iatros (physician) + genesis (origin) - disease originating from the doctor
related_words: iatrogène, médecin, complication, traitement, événement indésirable
common_mistakes: Iatrogénie (noun) vs iatrogène (adjective) - don't mix forms

primary_meaning: imponderable, a factor that cannot be foreseen or calculated
usage_notes: Often used in plural; use when discussing unknown factors in planning
common_collocations: facteur impondérable, élément impondérable, marge d'impondérables
synonyms: imprévu, inconnue, facteur incertain, variable imprévisible
image_context: Unpredictable elements and uncertain factors representing imponderables
example_1: Il faut toujours prévoir une marge pour les impondérables dans la planification.
example_1_translation: One must always plan for a margin for imponderables in planning.
example_2: Les impondérables climatiques compliquent sérieusement la planification agricole moderne.
example_2_translation: Climate imponderables seriously complicate modern agricultural planning.
example_3: Malgré tous nos calculs, certains impondérables ont affecté le résultat final.
example_3_translation: Despite all our calculations, certain imponderables affected the final result.
pronunciation_tips: in-pon-dé-rabl. Four syllables with stress on third: in-pon-DÉ-rabl
memory_hook: Impondérable - in (not) + pondérable (weighable) - not weighable or measurable
common_mistakes: Can be used as adjective too ('factor impondérable') but noun form is more common

primary_meaning: reification, treating an abstract concept as if it were concrete
secondary_meaning: the process of making something real or concrete
usage_notes: Use in philosophical analysis and critique of conceptual errors
common_collocations: réification d'abstractions, réification du concept, processus de réification
image_context: Abstract concepts becoming concrete things representing reification
example_1: Le concept de marché a été réifié par les économistes classiques dans leurs modèles.
example_1_translation: The concept of market was reified by classical economists in their models.
example_2: Réifier des abstractions conduit souvent à des erreurs graves de raisonnement.
example_2_translation: Reifying abstractions often leads to serious reasoning errors.
example_3: Cet argument commet l'erreur de réification en traitant la société comme une entité.
example_3_translation: This argument commits the error of reification by treating society as an entity.
pronunciation_tips: ré-i-fi-ka-sjON. Five syllables with stress on nasal ending
memory_hook: Réification - réifier (to make real) + fication (making) - making abstract things seem real
common_mistakes: Reification is often used as critique of faulty thinking - not neutral term
